title.zh: 卷五十五 衞青霍去病傳
title.en: Volume 55: Wei Qing and Huo Qubing
title.raw: 卷五十五 衞青霍去病傳 Volume 55: Wei Qing and Huo Qubing

sentences.zh: 衛青字仲卿。

translations.literal: Wei Qing, courtesy name Zhongqing.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing’s courtesy name was Zhongqing.

sentences.zh: 其父鄭季，河東平陽人也，以縣吏給事侯家。

translations.literal: His father Zheng Ji was a man of Pingyang in Hedong, and as a county clerk he served in a marquis’s household.
translations.idiomatic: His father, Zheng Ji, came from Pingyang in Hedong and had served as a county clerk in a marquis’s household.

sentences.zh: 平陽侯曹壽尚武帝姊陽信長公主。

translations.literal: Marquis of Pingyang Cao Shou had as his wife the Princess of Yangxin, the elder sister of Emperor Wu.
translations.idiomatic: The Marquis of Pingyang, Cao Shou, was married to the Princess of Yangxin, Emperor Wu’s elder sister.

sentences.zh: 季與主家僮衛媼通，生青。

translations.literal: Ji had relations with the bondservant Wei Ao of the princess’s household, and she bore Qing.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Ji took up with Wei Ao, a bondservant in the princess’s household, and she gave birth to Qing.

sentences.zh: 青有同母兄衛長及姊子夫，子夫自平陽公主家得幸武帝，故青冒姓為衛氏。

translations.literal: Qing had an elder brother by the same mother, Wei Zhang, and an elder sister, Zifu; Zifu, from the household of the Princess of Pingyang, won Emperor Wu’s favor, so Qing took the surname Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Qing had a maternal elder brother named Wei Zhang and an elder sister, Zifu. After Zifu, from the Princess of Pingyang’s household, became favored by Emperor Wu, Qing adopted the surname Wei.

sentences.zh: 衛媼長女君孺，次女少兒，次女則子夫。

translations.literal: Wei Ao’s eldest daughter was Junru, the next Shaor, and the next was Zifu.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Ao’s daughters were, in order: Junru, Shaor, and then Zifu.

sentences.zh: 子夫男弟步廣，皆冒衛氏。

translations.literal: Zifu’s younger brother by the same mother was Bu Guang; all of them took the surname Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Zifu’s younger brother by the same mother was Bu Guang; they all bore the surname Wei.

sentences.zh: 衛青為侯家人，少時歸其父，父使牧羊。

translations.literal: Wei Qing, as a member of a marquis’s household, in his youth went to live with his father, who set him to herd sheep.
translations.idiomatic: While Qing still lived as part of a marquis’s household, he was sent in boyhood to his father’s home, where his father put him to herding sheep.

sentences.zh: 民母之子皆奴畜之，不以為兄弟數。

translations.literal: The sons of his father’s wife treated him like a slave and did not count him among the brothers.
translations.idiomatic: His father’s legitimate sons treated him as little better than a slave and refused to acknowledge him as a brother.

sentences.zh: 青嘗從人至甘泉居室，有一鉗徒相青曰：「貴人也，官至封侯。」

translations.literal: Qing once followed someone to the Ganquan residence, where a man in fetters who read faces said to him, “You are a man of rank; you will rise to enfeoffment as a marquis.”
translations.idiomatic: Once, when Qing had accompanied someone to the Ganquan residence, a shackled convict skilled in physiognomy told him, “You are destined for nobility; your office will rise to a marquisate.”

sentences.zh: 青笑曰：「人奴之生，得無笞罵即足矣，安得封侯事乎！」

translations.literal: Qing laughed and said, “Born a slave’s son, if I escape beating and abuse that is enough; how could there be talk of a marquisate!”
translations.idiomatic: Qing laughed and replied, “I was born a slave’s son—as long as I am spared the rod and curses, I shall be content. What business have I with a marquisate?”

sentences.zh: 青壯，為侯家騎，從平陽主。

translations.literal: When Qing grew to manhood, he became a mounted attendant in the marquis’s household and attended the Princess of Pingyang.
translations.idiomatic: When he came of age, Qing served as a mounted attendant in the marquis’s household and rode with the Princess of Pingyang.

sentences.zh: 建元二年春，青姊子夫得入宮幸上。

translations.literal: In the spring of the second year of Jianyuan, Qing’s sister Zifu entered the palace and won the emperor’s favor.
translations.idiomatic: In the spring of the second year of Jianyuan (139 BCE), his sister Zifu entered the palace and became a favorite of the throne.

sentences.zh: 皇后，大長公主女也，無子，妒。

translations.literal: The empress was a daughter of the Grand Princess and had no son; she was jealous.
translations.idiomatic: The empress was the Grand Princess’s daughter; she had borne no heir and was consumed by jealousy.

sentences.zh: 大長公主聞衛子夫幸，有身，妒之，乃使人捕青。

translations.literal: When the Grand Princess heard that Wei Zifu was favored and pregnant, she was jealous and sent men to seize Qing.
translations.idiomatic: When the Grand Princess learned that Wei Zifu was with child and in favor, jealousy moved her to have Qing arrested.

sentences.zh: 青時給事建章，未知名。

translations.literal: At the time Qing was serving in the Jianzhang Palace and was not yet known by name.
translations.idiomatic: Qing was then on duty at the Jianzhang Palace and was still unknown to the wider court.

sentences.zh: 大長公主執囚青，欲殺之。

translations.literal: The Grand Princess had Qing seized and imprisoned, intending to kill him.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Princess had Qing bound and held in custody, intending to put him to death.

sentences.zh: 其友騎郎公孫敖與壯士往篡之，故得不死。

translations.literal: His friend the mounted attendant Gongsun Ao went with stalwarts and snatched him away, so he did not die.
translations.idiomatic: His friend Gongsun Ao, a mounted attendant, rallied bold men and rescued him by force, so he survived.

sentences.zh: 上聞，乃召青為建章監，侍中。

translations.literal: When the emperor heard of it, he summoned Qing and appointed him Jianzhang superintendent and palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor learned of this, he summoned Qing and named him superintendent of Jianzhang and a palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 及母昆弟貴，賞賜數日間累千金。

translations.literal: When his mother and siblings rose to honor, gifts and rewards ran to a thousand pounds of gold within a few days.
translations.idiomatic: Within days of his mother and siblings being raised to favor, gifts and rewards poured in until they amounted to a thousand pounds of gold.

sentences.zh: 君孺為太僕公孫賀妻。

translations.literal: Junru became the wife of Grand Coachman Gongsun He.
translations.idiomatic: Junru was married to Grand Coachman Gongsun He.

sentences.zh: 少兒故與陳掌通，上召貴掌。

translations.literal: Shaor had formerly been intimate with Chen Zhang; the emperor summoned Zhang and honored him.
translations.idiomatic: Shaor had previously been involved with Chen Zhang; the emperor called Zhang to court and ennobled him.

sentences.zh: 公孫敖由此益顯。

translations.literal: Thereby Gongsun Ao became more prominent.
translations.idiomatic: From that time Gongsun Ao’s standing grew.

sentences.zh: 子夫為夫人。

translations.literal: Zifu became a lady.
translations.idiomatic: Zifu was advanced to the rank of lady.

sentences.zh: 青為太中大夫。

translations.literal: Qing became grand counselor of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Qing was appointed grand counselor of the palace.

sentences.zh: 元光六年，拜為車騎將軍，擊匈奴，出上谷；

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Yuanguang, he was appointed chariot and cavalry general and struck the Xiongnu, marching out from Shanggu;
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year of Yuanguang (129 BCE), he was named chariot-and-cavalry general and led a strike against the Xiongnu, advancing from Shanggu;

sentences.zh: 公孫賀為輕車將軍，出雲中；

translations.literal: Gongsun He was light chariot general and marched out from Yunzhong;
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun He served as light-chariot general and issued from Yunzhong;

sentences.zh: 太中大夫公孫敖為騎將軍，出代郡；

translations.literal: Grand counselor of the palace Gongsun Ao was cavalry general and marched out from Daicommandery;
translations.idiomatic: Grand counselor of the palace Gongsun Ao was cavalry general and marched from Dai commandery;

sentences.zh: 衛尉李廣為驍騎將軍，出雁門：軍各萬騎。

translations.literal: Commandant of the guards Li Guang was swift cavalry general and marched out from Yanmen: each army had ten thousand horse.
translations.idiomatic: Commandant of the guards Li Guang was swift-cavalry general and advanced from Yanmen. Each column fielded ten thousand mounted men.

sentences.zh: 青至籠城，斬首虜數百。

translations.literal: Qing reached Longcheng and beheaded or captured several hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Qing reached Longcheng and took several hundred heads and prisoners.

sentences.zh: 騎將軍敖亡七千騎，衛尉廣為虜所得，得脫歸，皆當斬，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: Cavalry general Ao lost seven thousand horse; Commandant Guang was taken by the enemy but escaped and returned; all were liable for execution and ransomed their lives to become commoners.
translations.idiomatic: Cavalry general Gongsun Ao lost seven thousand horsemen; Commandant Li Guang fell into enemy hands but broke free and returned. Both faced capital sentence and commuted it by ransom, becoming commoners.

sentences.zh: 賀亦無功。

translations.literal: He also had no merit.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun He likewise achieved no distinction.

sentences.zh: 唯青賜爵關內侯。

translations.literal: Only Qing was granted rank as marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: Only Qing was enfeoffed with the rank of marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 是後匈奴仍侵犯邊。

translations.literal: After this the Xiongnu still repeatedly violated the border.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward the Xiongnu continued to raid the frontier.

sentences.zh: 語在匈奴傳。

translations.literal: The account is in the treatise on the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The details are given in the “Account of the Xiongnu.”

sentences.zh: 元朔五年春，令青將三萬騎出高闕，衛尉蘇建為遊擊將軍，左內史李沮為彊弩將軍，太僕公孫賀為騎將軍，代相李蔡為輕車將軍，皆領屬車騎將軍，俱出朔方。

translations.literal: In the spring of the fifth year of Yuanshuo, Qing was ordered to lead thirty thousand horse out of Gaoque; Commandant of the guards Su Jian was mobile-corps general, Director of the left interior Li Ju was strong crossbow general, Grand Coachman Gongsun He was cavalry general, Chancellor of Dai Li Cai was light chariot general—all were subordinate to the chariot and cavalry general—and together they marched out from Shuofang.
translations.idiomatic: In the spring of Yuanshuo 5 (124 BCE), the court ordered Qing to take thirty thousand cavalry through Gaoque, with Su Jian as mobile-corps general, Li Ju of the left interior as strong-crossbow general, Gongsun He as cavalry general, and Li Cai, chancellor of Dai, as light-chariot general, all under the chariot-and-cavalry general’s command, issuing together from Shuofang.

sentences.zh: 大行李息、岸頭侯張次公為將軍，俱出右北平。

translations.literal: Grand messenger Li Xi and Marquis of Antou Zhang Cigong were generals and together marched out from Youbeiping.
translations.idiomatic: Grand messenger Li Xi and Zhang Cigong, Marquis of Antou, held general’s commissions and advanced together from Youbeiping.

sentences.zh: 匈奴右賢王當青等兵，以為漢兵不能至此，飲醉，漢兵夜至，圍右賢王。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu right worthy king opposed Qing’s forces and, supposing Han troops could not reach there, drank himself drunk; Han troops came by night, surrounded the right worthy king.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu right worthy king lay athwart Qing’s line of march. Thinking Han forces could never reach him, he feasted until drunk; Han troops arrived in the night and ringed the right worthy king.

sentences.zh: 右賢王驚，夜逃，獨與其愛妾一人騎數百馳，潰圍北去。

translations.literal: The right worthy king was alarmed and fled by night, alone with one beloved concubine and several hundred riders, breaking the encirclement and fleeing north.
translations.idiomatic: The right worthy king panicked and fled under cover of darkness with only his favorite concubine and a few hundred horsemen, punching through the ring and galloping north.

sentences.zh: 漢輕騎校尉郭成等追數百里，弗得，得右賢裨王十餘人，眾男女萬五千餘人，畜數十百萬，於是引兵而還。

translations.literal: Han light cavalry colonels Guo Cheng and others pursued several hundred li but did not catch him; they took more than ten lieutenant kings subordinate to the right worthy, over fifteen thousand men and women of the horde, and livestock numbering in the tens of millions, then led the army back.
translations.idiomatic: Han light-cavalry colonels led by Guo Cheng chased them for several hundred li without overtaking him, but they captured more than ten petty kings under the right worthy, over fifteen thousand men and women, and livestock counted in the tens of millions, then withdrew.

sentences.zh: 至塞，天子使使者持大將軍印，即軍中拜青為大將軍，諸將皆以兵屬，立號而歸。

translations.literal: At the passes the Son of Heaven sent an envoy bearing the great general’s seal and, in the camp, invested Qing as great general; the generals all placed their troops under his command, assumed designations, and returned.
translations.idiomatic: At the frontier the emperor dispatched an envoy with the great general’s seal and, in the field, invested Qing as great general. The subordinate generals transferred their commands to him, took their new titles, and marched home.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「大將軍青躬率戎士，師大捷，獲匈奴王十有餘人，益封青八千七百戶。」

translations.literal: The emperor said, “Great General Qing personally led the warriors; the army won a great victory and captured more than ten Xiongnu kings; increase Qing’s fief by eight thousand seven hundred households.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor declared, “Great General Qing led the troops in person; the army scored a major victory and took more than ten Xiongnu kings. Add eight thousand seven hundred households to Qing’s fief.”

sentences.zh: 而封青子伉為宜春侯，子不疑為陰安侯，子登為發干侯。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed Qing’s son Kang as marquis of Yichun, son Buyi as marquis of Yin’an, and son Deng as marquis of Fagan.
translations.idiomatic: He enfeoffed Qing’s sons: Kang as marquis of Yichun, Buyi as marquis of Yin’an, and Deng as marquis of Fagan.

sentences.zh: 青固謝曰：「臣幸得待罪行間，賴陛下神靈，軍大捷，皆諸校力戰之功也。

translations.literal: Qing firmly declined, saying, “Your servant has been fortunate enough to await punishment in the ranks; relying on Your Majesty’s numinous power, the army won a great victory; it is all the merit of the colonels’ hard fighting.
translations.idiomatic: Qing pressed his refusal: “I have been privileged to serve in the ranks awaiting judgment; by Your Majesty’s divine favor the army triumphed—that is entirely the work of the colonels who fought to the limit.

sentences.zh: 陛下幸已益封臣青，臣青子在繈褓中，未有勤勞，上幸裂地封為三侯，非臣待罪行間所以勸士力戰之意也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty has already been pleased to enlarge your servant Qing’s fief; your servant Qing’s sons are still in swaddling clothes and have toiled not at all; Your Majesty’s favor in carving out territory to enfeoff three marquisates is not what your servant, awaiting punishment in the ranks, intends as encouragement for officers to fight hard.
translations.idiomatic: You have already graciously added to my fief; my sons are still in the cradle and have done no service. To split off lands and create three new marquisates for them is not how I, serving in the ranks under sentence, mean to spur men to battle.

sentences.zh: 伉等三人何敢受封！」

translations.literal: How dare Kang and the other three accept enfeoffment!”
translations.idiomatic: How could Kang and the other two dare accept such titles!”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「我非忘諸校功也，今固且圖之。」

translations.literal: The emperor said, “I have not forgotten the colonels’ merit; for now I am still planning it.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered, “I have not forgotten the colonels’ service; I am still working out their rewards.”

sentences.zh: 乃詔御史曰：「護軍都尉公孫敖三從大將軍擊匈奴，常護軍傅校獲王，封敖為合騎侯。

translations.literal: He then ordered the secretary to the board: “Commandant for the protection of the army Gongsun Ao thrice followed the great general in striking the Xiongnu; he constantly guarded the army, attended the colonels, and captured a king—enfeoff Ao as marquis of Heqi.
translations.idiomatic: He then instructed the imperial secretary: “Commandant for the protection of the army Gongsun Ao accompanied the great general on three campaigns against the Xiongnu, consistently shielding the host and escorting the colonels until a king was taken—invest him as marquis of Heqi.

sentences.zh: 都尉韓說從大軍出窴渾，至匈奴右賢王庭，為戲下搏戰獲王，封說為龍哣侯。

translations.literal: Commandant Han Shuo followed the great army out of Tianhun, reached the court of the Xiongnu right worthy king, fought hand-to-hand under the commander’s banner and captured a king—enfeoff Shuo as marquis of Longkou.
translations.idiomatic: Commandant Han Shuo marched with the main force through Tianhun to the court of the Xiongnu right worthy king, closed in combat beneath the general’s standard, and seized a king—invest him as marquis of Longkou.

sentences.zh: 騎將軍賀從大將軍獲王，封賀為南窌侯。

translations.literal: Cavalry general He followed the great general and captured a king—enfeoff He as marquis of Nanjiao.
translations.idiomatic: Cavalry general Gongsun He followed the great general and took a king—invest him as marquis of Nanjiao.

sentences.zh: 輕車將軍李蔡再從大將軍獲王，封蔡為樂安侯。

translations.literal: Light chariot general Li Cai twice followed the great general and captured a king—enfeoff Cai as marquis of Le’an.
translations.idiomatic: Light-chariot general Li Cai twice accompanied the great general and captured a king—invest him as marquis of Le’an.

sentences.zh: 校尉李朔、趙不虞、公孫戎奴各三從大將軍獲王，封朔為陟軹侯，不虞為隨成侯，戎奴為從平侯。

translations.literal: Colonels Li Shuo, Zhao Buyu, and Gongsun Rongnu each thrice followed the great general and captured a king—enfeoff Shuo as marquis of Zhizhi, Buyu as marquis of Suicheng, and Rongnu as marquis of Congping.
translations.idiomatic: Colonels Li Shuo, Zhao Buyu, and Gongsun Rongnu each went three times with the great general and brought in a king—invest Li Shuo as marquis of Zhizhi, Zhao Buyu as marquis of Suicheng, and Gongsun Rongnu as marquis of Congping.

sentences.zh: 將軍李沮、李息及校尉豆如意、中郎將綰皆有功，賜爵關內侯。

translations.literal: Generals Li Ju and Li Xi and colonels Dou Ruyi and Zhonglang general Wan all had merit and were granted rank as marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: Generals Li Ju and Li Xi, colonel Dou Ruyi, and Zhonglang general Wan all earned distinction and received the rank of marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 沮、息、如意食邑各三百戶。」

translations.literal: Ju, Xi, and Ruyi each received three hundred households of revenue.”
translations.idiomatic: Li Ju, Li Xi, and Dou Ruyi were each granted three hundred households of income.”

sentences.zh: 其秋，匈奴入代，殺都尉。

translations.literal: That autumn the Xiongnu entered Dai and killed the commandant.
translations.idiomatic: That autumn the Xiongnu invaded Dai and slew the commandant.

sentences.zh: 明年春，大將軍青出定襄，合騎侯敖為中將軍，太僕賀為左將軍，翕侯趙信為前將軍，衛尉蘇建為右將軍，郎中令李廣為後將軍，左內史李沮為彊弩將軍，咸屬大將軍，斬首數千級而還。

translations.literal: The next spring the great general Qing marched out of Dingxiang; Marquis of Heqi Ao was central general, Grand Coachman He was left general, Marquis of Xi Zhao Xin was van general, Commandant of the guards Su Jian was right general, Zhonglang commandant Li Guang was rear general, Director of the left interior Li Ju was strong crossbow general—all were subordinate to the great general—and they took several thousand heads by rank and returned.
translations.idiomatic: The following spring the great general issued from Dingxiang with Gongsun Ao as central general, Gongsun He as left general, the Marquis of Xi Zhao Xin as van, Su Jian as right general, Zhonglang commandant Li Guang as rear, and Li Ju of the left interior as strong-crossbow general, all under the great general’s command; they took several thousand heads and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 月餘，悉復出定襄，斬首虜萬餘人。

translations.literal: After a month or more they all again marched out of Dingxiang and beheaded or captured over ten thousand enemy.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a month later they again sortied from Dingxiang and killed or captured more than ten thousand.

sentences.zh: 蘇建、趙信并軍三千餘騎，獨逢單于兵，與戰一日餘，漢兵且盡。

translations.literal: Su Jian and Zhao Xin combined their forces at just over three thousand horse and alone encountered the shanyu’s army; they fought for more than a day and the Han troops were nearly gone.
translations.idiomatic: Su Jian and Zhao Xin, with a little over three thousand horsemen between them, ran headlong into the shanyu’s host and fought for more than a day until the Han detachment was all but annihilated.

sentences.zh: 信故胡人，降為翕侯，見急，匈奴誘之，遂將其餘騎可八百奔降單于。

translations.literal: Xin had formerly been a Hu who had surrendered and been made Marquis of Xi; seeing the crisis, the Xiongnu enticed him, and he then led his remaining horse, about eight hundred, in flight to surrender to the shanyu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Xin was a Hu who had surrendered and been enfeoffed as Marquis of Xi; pressed to extremity, he yielded to Xiongnu blandishments and led the eight hundred or so riders left to him in desertion to the shanyu.

sentences.zh: 蘇建盡亡其軍，獨以身得亡去，自歸青。

translations.literal: Su Jian lost his entire army and alone escaped in person, returning of his own accord to Qing.
translations.idiomatic: Su Jian’s command was wiped out; he alone got away and presented himself to Qing.

sentences.zh: 青問其罪正閎、長史安、議郎周霸等：「建當云何？」

translations.literal: Qing asked his crime to Zheng Hong the adjutant, chief clerk An, advisory counselor Zhou Ba, and others: “What should be done with Jian?”
translations.idiomatic: Qing put the question of Jian’s offense to colonel for discipline Zheng Hong, chief clerk An, advisory counselor Zhou Ba, and the rest: “What sentence does Jian deserve?”

sentences.zh: 霸曰：「自大將軍出，未嘗斬裨將，今建棄軍，可斬，以明將軍之威。」

translations.literal: Ba said, “Since the great general marched out, he has never beheaded a lieutenant general; now Jian abandoned his army—he can be beheaded to make clear the general’s awesomeness.”
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ba said, “Since the great general took the field he has never executed a lieutenant general. Jian threw away his command—execute him to establish the general’s authority.”

sentences.zh: 閎、安曰：「不然。

translations.literal: Hong and An said, “It is not so.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Hong and chief clerk An replied, “No.

sentences.zh: 兵法『小敵之堅，大敵之禽也。』

translations.literal: The Art of War says, ‘When a small enemy holds firm, it becomes the catch of a large enemy.’
translations.idiomatic: The Art of War says, ‘Stubborn resistance by the weak delivers them into the hands of the strong.’

sentences.zh: 今建以數千當單于數萬，力戰一日餘，士皆不敢有二心。

translations.literal: Now Jian with several thousand opposed the shanyu’s tens of thousands, fought hard for more than a day, and the men never dared harbor a double heart.
translations.idiomatic: Jian held several thousand against the shanyu’s tens of thousands and fought a full day and more without his men wavering.

sentences.zh: 自歸而斬之，是示後無反意也。

translations.literal: To behead him when he has returned of his own accord is to show those who come after that there is no point in coming back.
translations.idiomatic: To cut off his head after he made his own way back would signal to future officers that there is no reward in returning.

sentences.zh: 不當斬。」

translations.literal: He should not be beheaded.”
translations.idiomatic: He ought not to be executed.”

sentences.zh: 青曰：「青幸得以胏附待罪行間，不患無威，而霸說我以明威，甚失臣意。

translations.literal: Qing said, “Qing has been fortunate enough to wait in the ranks as flesh-and-bone kin; I do not worry about lacking awesomeness, yet Ba urges me to make awesomeness clear—this greatly misses the intent of a minister.
translations.idiomatic: Qing said, “I have been privileged to serve in the ranks as an imperial in-law; I need no borrowed severity, yet Zhou Ba would have me strike fear into the army—that is far from how a subject should think.

sentences.zh: 且使臣職雖當斬將，以臣之尊寵而不敢自擅專誅於境外，其歸天子，天子自裁之，於以風為人臣不敢專權，不亦可乎？」

translations.literal: Moreover, though in my duty I might be entitled to behead a general, given my honor and favor I dare not on my own authority carry out execution beyond the border; I will send him back to the Son of Heaven and let the Son of Heaven decide—in this way making the point that ministers dare not monopolize power—is that not acceptable?”
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, even if my office authorized me to execute a general, my rank and favor forbid me to kill on my own beyond the frontier. I will remit Jian to the emperor and let the throne decide—thus teaching that no subject may arrogate the power of life and death. Is that not the right course?”

sentences.zh: 軍吏皆曰「善」。

translations.literal: The officers all said “Good.”
translations.idiomatic: The staff officers all said, “It is right.”

sentences.zh: 遂囚建行在所。

translations.literal: They then imprisoned Jian at the traveling encampment.
translations.idiomatic: They therefore held Jian in custody at the imperial field headquarters.

sentences.zh: 是歲也，霍去病始侯。

translations.literal: In that year Huo Qubing first received a marquisate.
translations.idiomatic: That same year Huo Qubing was first enfeoffed as a marquis.

sentences.zh: 霍去病

translations.literal: Huo Qubing
translations.idiomatic: Huo Qubing

sentences.zh: 霍去病，大將軍青姊少兒子也。

translations.literal: Huo Qubing was the son of the great general Qing’s elder sister Shaor.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Qubing was the son of Qing’s elder sister Shaor.

sentences.zh: 其父霍仲孺先與少兒通，生去病。

translations.literal: His father Huo Zhongru had formerly been intimate with Shaor and begat Qubing.
translations.idiomatic: His father, Huo Zhongru, had earlier been involved with Shaor and fathered Qubing.

sentences.zh: 及衛皇后尊，少兒更為詹事陳掌妻。

translations.literal: When Empress Wei rose to honor, Shaor was remarried as the wife of Chen Zhang the chamberlain for the palace.
translations.idiomatic: After Empress Wei gained prominence, Shaor was given in marriage to Chen Zhang, who served as chamberlain for the palace.

sentences.zh: 去病以皇后姊子，年十八為侍中。

translations.literal: Qubing, as the empress’s elder sister’s son, at age eighteen became a palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: As the empress’s nephew, Qubing was made a palace attendant at eighteen.

sentences.zh: 善騎射，再從大將軍。

translations.literal: He was skilled at riding and shooting and twice followed the great general.
translations.idiomatic: He excelled in horsemanship and archery and twice campaigned with the great general.

sentences.zh: 大將軍受詔，予壯士，為票姚校尉，與輕勇騎八百直棄大將軍數百里赴利，斬捕首虜過當。

translations.literal: The great general received an edict, gave him picked warriors, made him colonel of agile raiders, and with eight hundred light and brave horse he went straight several hundred li from the great general to seize advantage, beheading and capturing beyond the norm.
translations.idiomatic: The great general, acting on imperial orders, detached picked troops and appointed him colonel of agile raiders; with eight hundred swift, daring horsemen he drove several hundred li ahead of the main column to strike for gain, taking heads and prisoners far in excess of his numbers.

sentences.zh: 於是上曰：「票姚校尉去病斬首捕虜二千二十八級，得相國、當戶，斬單于大父行藉若侯產，捕季父羅姑比，再冠軍，以二千五百戶封去病為冠軍侯。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor said, “Colonel of agile raiders Qubing beheaded or captured 2,028, took a chancellor and a household head, beheaded Chan, Marquis of Jiru in the shanyu’s grandfather’s generation, captured the shanyu’s uncle by the father Luogubi, twice outshone all others in merit, and with 2,500 households enfeoff Qubing as marquis of Champion.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor therefore announced, “Colonel of agile raiders Huo Qubing slew or captured two thousand twenty-eight, seized a chancellor and a household head, cut down Jiru Marquis Chan of the shanyu’s grandfather’s line, and took his uncle by the father Luogubi; twice he has led all in merit—invest him with two thousand five hundred households as marquis of Champion.

sentences.zh: 上谷太守郝賢四從大將軍，捕首虜千三百級，封賢為終利侯。

translations.literal: Prefect of Shanggu Hao Xian four times followed the great general, captured 1,300 heads, and was enfeoffed Xian as marquis of Zhongli.
translations.idiomatic: Hao Xian, prefect of Shanggu, accompanied the great general on four expeditions, took thirteen hundred heads, and was enfeoffed as marquis of Zhongli.

sentences.zh: 騎士孟已有功，賜爵關內侯，邑二百戶。」

translations.literal: Mounted warrior Meng Yi had merit and was granted rank as marquis within the passes with two hundred households.”
translations.idiomatic: The mounted warrior Meng Yi also earned distinction and received the rank of marquis within the passes with an income of two hundred households.”

sentences.zh: 是歲失兩將軍，亡翕侯，功不多，故青不益封。

translations.literal: That year two generals were lost and the Marquis of Xi was gone; achievements were not great, so Qing’s fief was not increased.
translations.idiomatic: That campaign cost two generals and the defection of the Marquis of Xi; gains were modest, so Qing received no addition to his fief.

sentences.zh: 蘇建至，上弗誅，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: When Su Jian arrived, the emperor did not execute him; he ransomed his life and became a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: When Su Jian reached the capital, the emperor spared his life; he commuted sentence by ransom and was reduced to commoner status.

sentences.zh: 青賜千金。

translations.literal: Qing was gifted a thousand pounds of gold.
translations.idiomatic: The court presented Qing with a thousand pounds of gold.

sentences.zh: 是時王夫人方幸於上，甯乘說青曰：「將軍所以功未甚多，身食萬戶，三子皆為侯者，以皇后故也。

translations.literal: At that time Lady Wang was just then favored by the emperor; Ning Cheng said to Qing, “The reason the general’s merit is not yet very great yet his person enjoys ten thousand households and three sons are all marquises is because of the empress.
translations.idiomatic: About then Lady Wang had newly won the emperor’s favor. Ning Cheng told Qing, “Your battlefield laurels are still modest, yet you hold a ten-thousand-household income and all three sons are marquises—that rests on the empress’s position.

sentences.zh: 今王夫人幸而宗族未富貴，願將軍奉所賜千金為王夫人親壽。」

translations.literal: Now Lady Wang is favored yet her kin are not yet wealthy and honored; I beg the general to offer the gifted thousand pounds of gold to celebrate a birthday for Lady Wang’s mother.”
translations.idiomatic: Lady Wang is rising in favor while her clan still lacks rank and wealth. I urge you to devote part of that imperial gift—a thousand pounds of gold—to toast the longevity of Lady Wang’s mother.”

sentences.zh: 青以五百金為王夫人親壽。

translations.literal: Qing used five hundred pounds of gold to celebrate a birthday for Lady Wang’s mother.
translations.idiomatic: Qing accordingly sent five hundred pounds of gold for Lady Wang’s mother’s birthday.

sentences.zh: 上聞，問青，青以實對。

translations.literal: When the emperor heard of it, he asked Qing, and Qing answered with the truth.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor heard of it, he questioned Qing, who reported the facts.

sentences.zh: 上乃拜甯乘為東海都尉。

translations.literal: The emperor then appointed Ning Cheng commandant of the east sea.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor thereupon named Ning Cheng commandant of the Eastern Sea.

sentences.zh: 校尉張騫從大將軍，以嘗使大夏，留匈奴中久，道軍，知善水草處，軍得以無飢渴，因前使絕國功，封騫為博望侯。

translations.literal: Colonel Zhang Qian followed the great general; because he had once been sent to Bactria and stayed long among the Xiongnu, he guided the army and knew where the grass and water were good, so the army suffered neither hunger nor thirst; on account of his former merit in opening states beyond the frontier, Qian was enfeoffed as marquis of Bowang.
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Zhang Qian marched with the great general. Having once been dispatched to Daxia and detained long among the Xiongnu, he showed the army the way and knew where forage and water lay, so the troops went neither hungry nor thirsty. For his earlier service in piercing the barrier to distant realms he was invested as marquis of Bowang.

sentences.zh: 去病侯三歲，元狩三年春為票騎將軍，將萬騎出隴西，有功。

translations.literal: Three years after Qubing’s marquisate, in the spring of the third year of Yuanshou, he became swift cavalry general, led ten thousand horse out of Longxi, and had merit.
translations.idiomatic: Three years after his enfeoffment, in the spring of Yuanshou 3 (120 BCE), he was named swift-cavalry general, led ten thousand cavalry out of Longxi, and distinguished himself.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「票騎將軍率戎士隃烏盭，討遫濮，涉狐奴，歷五王國，輜重人眾攝讋者弗取，幾獲單于子。

translations.literal: The emperor said, “Swift cavalry general led the warriors across Wuli, attacked Chipu, forded Hunu, passed through five kingdoms; baggage and people who submitted in fear he did not seize; he nearly captured the shanyu’s son.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor declared, “The swift-cavalry general drove his men over Wuli, struck Chipu, crossed Hunu, and traversed five kingdoms; he left baggage trains and frightened common folk untouched and nearly bagged the shanyu’s heir.

sentences.zh: 轉戰六日，過焉支山千有餘里，合短兵，鏖皋蘭下，殺折蘭王，斬盧侯王，銳悍者誅，全甲獲醜，執渾邪王子及相國、都尉，捷首虜八千九百六十級，收休屠祭天金人，師率減什七，益封去病二千二百戶。」

translations.literal: He fought for six days, crossed Mount Yanzhi more than a thousand li, closed with short weapons, fought fiercely below Mount Gaolan, killed King Zhelan, beheaded King Luhou, put to death those who were fierce and brave, took armor whole and captives in numbers, seized the shanyu’s son of King Hunye, the chancellor, and the commandant, slew or captured 8,960, took the golden man with which King Xiutu sacrificed to Heaven, and his command lost seven tenths of its strength—increase Qubing’s fief by 2,200 households.”
translations.idiomatic: For six days he carried the fight, crossed Yanzhi Mountain well over a thousand li, closed to sword’s length below Mount Gaolan, slew King Zhelan, cut down King Luhou, executed the stubborn fighters, captured mail and prisoners intact, took the shanyu’s son of King Hunye together with a chancellor and commandant, counted eight thousand nine hundred sixty heads and captives, seized the golden idol King Xiutu used for Heaven worship, and lost seven men in ten from his own ranks—add two thousand two hundred households to Qubing’s fief.”

sentences.zh: 其夏，去病與合騎侯敖俱出北地，異道。

translations.literal: That summer Qubing and Marquis of Heqi Ao both marched out of Beidi by different routes.
translations.idiomatic: That summer Qubing and Marquis of Heqi Gongsun Ao both advanced from Beidi by separate columns.

sentences.zh: 博望侯張騫、郎中令李廣俱出右北平，異道。

translations.literal: Marquis of Bowang Zhang Qian and Zhonglang commandant Li Guang both marched out of Youbeiping by different routes.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis of Bowang Zhang Qian and Zhonglang commandant Li Guang likewise issued from Youbeiping by different routes.

sentences.zh: 廣將四千騎先至，騫將萬騎後。

translations.literal: Guang led four thousand horse and arrived first; Qian led ten thousand horse and came after.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang with four thousand horsemen reached the field first; Zhang Qian followed with ten thousand.

sentences.zh: 匈奴左賢王將數萬騎圍廣，廣與戰二日，死者過半，所殺亦過當。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu left worthy king led several ten thousand horse and surrounded Guang; Guang fought for two days, the dead were more than half, those killed on the other side were also beyond proportion.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu left worthy king brought several tens of thousands of riders and surrounded Li Guang. For two days Guang fought on; he lost more than half his men but inflicted casualties equally out of proportion.

sentences.zh: 騫至，匈奴引兵去。

translations.literal: When Qian arrived, the Xiongnu led the troops away.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Qian arrived, the Xiongnu lifted the siege and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 騫坐行留，當斬，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: Qian was liable for execution for proceeding slowly; he ransomed his life and became a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian was sentenced to death for dilatory marching; he bought off execution and was reduced to commoner status.

sentences.zh: 而去病出北地，遂深入，合騎侯失道，不相得。

translations.literal: But Qubing marched out of Beidi, went deep in; Marquis of Heqi lost the way and they did not link up.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Qubing pushed out from Beidi deep into the steppe; Marquis of Heqi Gongsun Ao missed his route and never joined him.

sentences.zh: 去病至祁連山，捕首虜甚多。

translations.literal: Qubing reached the Qilian Mountains and captured very many heads and prisoners.
translations.idiomatic: Qubing reached the Qilian range and took a very large toll of heads and captives.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「票騎將軍涉鈞耆，濟居延，遂臻小月氏，攻祁連山，揚武乎鱳得，得單于單桓、酋涂王，及相國、都尉以眾降下者二千五百人，可謂能舍服知成而止矣。

translations.literal: The emperor said, “The swift-cavalry general crossed Junqi, forded Juyan, reached the Lesser Yuezhi, attacked the Qilian Mountains, displayed arms at Lude, took the shanyu’s kings Danhuan and Qiutu, and chancellors and commandants with the masses who surrendered—two thousand five hundred persons; he may be called one who knows how to spare those who submit, perceives when success is won, and stops.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor declared, “The swift-cavalry general crossed Junqi, crossed Juyan, advanced to the Lesser Yuezhi, stormed the Qilian line, struck fear at Lude, and brought in the shanyu’s kings Danhuan and Qiutu, together with chancellors, commandants, and more than two thousand five hundred men of their following who yielded. Here is a commander who knows when to accept surrender, recognize a settled victory, and halt.”

sentences.zh: 捷首虜三萬二百，獲五王，王母、單于閼氏、王子五十九人，相國、將軍、當戶、都尉六十三人，師大率減什三，益封去病五千四百戶。

translations.literal: He took heads and prisoners 30,200, captured five kings, the queen mother, the shanyu’s chief wife, and fifty-nine princes, sixty-three chancellors, generals, household heads, and commandants; the army lost on the whole three tenths of its strength—increase Qubing’s fief by 5,400 households.
translations.idiomatic: The butcher’s bill ran to thirty thousand two hundred heads and prisoners, five kings, a queen mother, a shanyu consort, fifty-nine princes, and sixty-three chancellors, generals, household heads, and commandants. Casualties ran to about three in ten—increase Huo Qubing’s fief by fifty-four hundred households.

sentences.zh: 賜校尉從至小月氏者爵左庶長。

translations.literal: He granted the rank of left chief of the people to colonels who had followed him to the Lesser Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: Colonels who had accompanied him to the Lesser Yuezhi were raised to the rank of left chief of the people.

sentences.zh: 鷹擊司馬破奴再從票騎將軍斬遫濮王，捕稽且王，右千騎將王、王母各一人，王子以下四十一人，捕虜三千三百三十人，前行捕虜千四百人，封破奴為從票侯。

translations.literal: Eagle-strike major Ponu twice followed the swift-cavalry general, beheaded King Chipu, captured King Jiju, one king and one queen mother of the right thousand-cavalry general, forty-one persons from princes downward, captured 3,330 prisoners, and in the van took 1,400 prisoners—enfeoff Ponu as marquis attendant on the swift-cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Major Ponu of the eagle-strike corps twice rode with the swift-cavalry general: he slew King Chipu, seized King Jiju, took a king and queen mother of the right thousand-cavalry command, forty-one nobles below the rank of prince, thirty-three hundred thirty captives, and fourteen hundred more in the lead column—invest Ponu as marquis attendant on the swift-cavalry.

sentences.zh: 校尉高不識從票騎將軍捕呼于耆王王子以下十一人，捕虜千七百六十八人，封不識為宜冠侯。

translations.literal: Colonel Gao Bushi followed the swift-cavalry general, captured King Huyuqi and eleven persons from princes downward, captured 1,768 prisoners—enfeoff Bushi as marquis of Yiguan.
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Gao Bushi marched with the swift-cavalry general, seized King Huyuqi and eleven princes and nobles beneath him, and seventeen hundred sixty-eight captives—invest Bushi as marquis of Yiguan.

sentences.zh: 校尉僕多有功，封為煇渠侯。」

translations.literal: Colonel Puyou had merit and was enfeoffed as marquis of Huiqu.”
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Puyou also earned distinction and was enfeoffed as marquis of Huiqu.”

sentences.zh: 合騎侯敖坐留不與票騎將軍會，當斬，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: Marquis of Heqi Ao, sitting for delay and failing to join the swift-cavalry general, was liable for execution; he ransomed his life and became a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis of Heqi Gongsun Ao was sentenced to death for loitering and missing his rendezvous with the swift-cavalry general; he commuted the sentence and was stripped to commoner status.

sentences.zh: 諸宿將所將士馬兵亦不如去病，去病所將常選，然亦敢深入，常與壯騎先其大軍，軍亦有天幸，未嘗困絕也。

translations.literal: The officers, mounts, and men led by the veteran generals were also not the equal of Qubing’s; those Qubing led were always picked, yet he also dared to go deep, constantly with picked horse riding ahead of the great army, and the army also had Heaven’s luck and never was cut off in extremity.
translations.idiomatic: The veteran generals could not match the quality of his troops—Qubing habitually led an elite corps—yet he still drove deep, usually racing ahead of the main body with picked riders, and his columns enjoyed a kind of fortune that never left them trapped or destitute.

sentences.zh: 然而諸宿將常留落不耦。

translations.literal: Yet the veteran generals often lagged behind and did not pair with him.
translations.idiomatic: The older commanders, by contrast, habitually fell behind and failed to keep pace with him.

sentences.zh: 由此去病日以親貴，比大將軍。

translations.literal: From this Qubing daily grew closer in favor and honor, comparable to the great general.
translations.idiomatic: Henceforward Qubing rose day by day in imperial favor until he stood on a par with the great general.

sentences.zh: 其後，單于怒渾邪王居西方數為漢所破，亡數萬人，以票騎之兵也，欲召誅渾邪王。

translations.literal: Afterward the shanyu was angry that King Hunyex, stationed in the west, had several times been broken by Han and had lost several tens of thousands of men to the swift-cavalry’s troops, and wished to summon and execute King Hunyex.
translations.idiomatic: Later the shanyu, furious that King Hunyex in the west had been repeatedly shattered by Han and had lost tens of thousands of men to the swift-cavalry columns, resolved to summon him and put him to death.

sentences.zh: 渾邪王與休屠王等謀欲降漢，使人先要道邊。

translations.literal: King Hunyex with King Xiutu and others plotted to surrender to Han and sent men first to signal the border along the road.
translations.idiomatic: King Hunyex conspired with King Xiutu and others to defect to Han and dispatched envoys to clear the way with the frontier posts.

sentences.zh: 是時大行李息將城河上，得渾邪王使，即馳傳以聞。

translations.literal: At that time Grand Messenger Li Xi was walling the Yellow River and captured King Hunyex’s envoy, immediately relaying post-horses to report.
translations.idiomatic: Grand messenger Li Xi was then fortifying the Yellow River line; he intercepted King Hunyex’s messenger and rushed the news to court by post-station relay.

sentences.zh: 上恐其以詐降而襲邊，乃令去病將兵往迎之。

translations.literal: The emperor feared they might feign surrender and raid the border, and ordered Qubing to lead troops to go welcome them.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing a false capitulation meant to ambush the frontier, the emperor ordered Qubing to take an army forward to receive them.

sentences.zh: 去病既度河，與渾邪眾相望。

translations.literal: Qubing, having crossed the river, was within sight of Hunyex’s horde.
translations.idiomatic: Once Qubing had crossed the river, he drew up in full view of King Hunyex’s host.

sentences.zh: 渾邪裨王將見漢軍而多欲不降者，頗遁去。

translations.literal: Hunyex lieutenant kings who were to lead the meeting with Han troops and among whom many did not wish to surrender for the most part fled.
translations.idiomatic: Among the lieutenant kings slated to parley with the Han, many had second thoughts about surrender and slipped away in large numbers.

sentences.zh: 去病乃馳入，得與渾邪王相見，斬其欲亡者八千人，遂獨遣渾邪王乘傳先詣行在所，盡將其眾度河，降者數萬人，號稱十萬。

translations.literal: Qubing then galloped in, was able to meet King Hunyex face to face, beheaded eight thousand of those who wished to flee, then alone sent King Hunyex riding post-carriages first to the traveling encampment, led all his masses across the river—several tens of thousands who surrendered, claiming a hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Qubing spurred straight into the column, met King Hunyex in person, and cut down eight thousand would-be deserters. He then put the king on relay carriages to the emperor’s field headquarters, shepherded the entire following across the river—several tens of thousands of capitulators who boasted their number at a hundred thousand.

sentences.zh: 既至長安，天子所以賞賜數十鉅萬。

translations.literal: When they reached Chang’an, what the Son of Heaven granted in rewards ran to several hundred million cash.
translations.idiomatic: At Chang’an the emperor’s gifts ran into the hundreds of millions.

sentences.zh: 封渾邪王萬戶，為漯陰侯。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed King Hunyex with ten thousand households as marquis of Luoyin.
translations.idiomatic: He invested King Hunyex with ten thousand households as marquis of Luoyin.

sentences.zh: 封其裨王呼毒尼為下摩侯，雁疪為煇渠侯，禽黎為河綦侯，大當戶調雖為常樂侯。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed his lieutenant kings: Hudu’ni as marquis of Xiamo, Yanzhi as marquis of Huiqu, Qinli as marquis of Heqi, grand household head Diaosui as marquis of Changle.
translations.idiomatic: His lieutenant kings received titles as well: Hudu’ni as marquis of Xiamo, Yanzhi as marquis of Huiqu, Qinli as marquis of Heqi, and grand household head Diaosui as marquis of Changle.

sentences.zh: 於是上嘉去病之功，曰：「票騎將軍去病率師征匈奴，西域王渾邪王及厥眾萌咸奔於率，以軍糧接食，并將控弦萬有餘人，誅獟悍，捷首虜八千餘級，降異國之王三十二。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor praised Qubing’s merit, saying, “Swift-cavalry general Qubing led the army to chastise the Xiongnu; King Hunyex of the Western Regions and his dependent people all came in submission at the head, were fed with army grain, and together brought more than ten thousand bowmen; he executed the fierce and bold, took more than eight thousand heads and prisoners, and reduced thirty-two kings of foreign states.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then extolled Qubing: “The swift-cavalry general led the host against the Xiongnu; King Hunyex of the Western Regions and his followers submitted in a body, drew rations from our baggage train, and mustered more than ten thousand archers. He cut down the die-hards, counted more than eight thousand heads and captives, and brought thirty-two foreign kings to heel.”

sentences.zh: 戰士不離傷，十萬之眾畢懷集服。

translations.literal: The fighting men neither scattered nor suffered grave harm; the horde of a hundred thousand all submitted in gathering allegiance.
translations.idiomatic: His men held formation with light losses, and the vast host that had marched in now submitted as one.

sentences.zh: 仍興之勞，爰及河塞，庶幾亡患。

translations.literal: The labor of this undertaking extended to the passes of the Yellow River, so that perhaps there would be no further peril.
translations.idiomatic: The effort reached the river frontier, easing the threat along the northern line.

sentences.zh: 以千七百戶益封票騎將軍。

translations.literal: Increase the swift-cavalry general’s fief by 1,700 households.
translations.idiomatic: Add seventeen hundred households to the swift-cavalry general’s fief.

sentences.zh: 減隴西、北地、上郡戍卒之半，以寬天下繇役。」

translations.literal: Halve the garrison troops of Longxi, Beidi, and Shang commanderies, so as to lighten corvée labor for the realm.”
translations.idiomatic: Cut the standing garrisons of Longxi, Beidi, and Shang by half to spare the empire’s labor levies.”

sentences.zh: 乃分處降者於邊五郡故塞外，而皆在河南，因其故俗為屬國。

translations.literal: He then apportioned the surrendered people beyond the old barrier of five border commanderies, all south of the river, and according to their former customs made them dependent states.
translations.idiomatic: The capitulators were resettled south of the river beyond the old wall of five frontier commanderies and organized as dependent states under their own customs.

sentences.zh: 其明年，匈奴入右北平、定襄，殺略漢千餘人。

translations.literal: The next year the Xiongnu entered Youbeiping and Dingxiang and killed or carried off more than a thousand Han.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Xiongnu raiders struck Youbeiping and Dingxiang, killing or capturing over a thousand Han subjects.

sentences.zh: 其明年，上與諸將議曰：「翕侯趙信為單于畫計，常以為漢兵不能度幕輕留，今大發卒，其勢必得所欲。」

translations.literal: The next year the emperor deliberated with the generals, saying, “Marquis of Xi Zhao Xin has drawn plans for the shanyu, constantly supposing Han troops cannot cross the desert in light strength and linger; now if we mobilize a great host, the situation is sure to achieve what we desire.”
translations.idiomatic: The next spring the emperor told his generals, “Zhao Xin the Marquis of Xi has advised the shanyu that Han armies cannot cross the desert in light order and remain there. If we now raise a massive force, we shall finally get what we want.”

sentences.zh: 是歲元狩四年也。

translations.literal: That year was the fourth year of Yuanshou.
translations.idiomatic: The year was Yuanshou 4 (119 BCE).

sentences.zh: 春，上令大將軍青、票騎將軍去病各五萬騎，步兵轉者踵軍數十萬，而敢力戰深入之士皆屬去病。

translations.literal: In spring the emperor ordered Great General Qing and Swift-cavalry General Qubing each to have fifty thousand horse, foot soldiers and transport following the army numbering several hundred thousand, and the men who dared to fight hard and go deep all were assigned to Qubing.
translations.idiomatic: That spring he gave each of the great general and the swift-cavalry general fifty thousand horsemen, with hundreds of thousands of infantry and train troops in their wake, while every officer eager to strike deep was placed under Qubing.

sentences.zh: 去病始為出定襄，當單于。

translations.literal: Qubing at first was to march out of Dingxiang and confront the shanyu.
translations.idiomatic: Qubing was first slated to issue from Dingxiang and take the shanyu head-on.

sentences.zh: 捕虜，虜言單于東，乃更令去病出代郡，令青出定襄。

translations.literal: Captives said the shanyu was to the east, so he changed orders and had Qubing march out of Daicommandery and Qing out of Dingxiang.
translations.idiomatic: Prisoners reported the shanyu lay to the east, so the plan was reversed: Qubing would sortie from Dai while Qing advanced from Dingxiang.

sentences.zh: 郎中令李廣為前將軍，太僕公孫賀為左將軍，主爵趙食其為右將軍，平陽侯襄為後將軍，皆屬大將軍。

translations.literal: Zhonglang commandant Li Guang was van general, Grand Coachman Gongsun He was left general, chief of the bureau of nobility Zhao Shiyi was right general, Marquis of Pingyang Xiang was rear general—all were subordinate to the great general.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang became van general, Gongsun He left general, Zhao Shiyi of the nobility bureau right general, and Xiang the Marquis of Pingyang rear general, all under the great general’s command.

sentences.zh: 趙信為單于謀曰：「漢兵即度幕，人馬罷，匈奴可坐收虜耳。」

translations.literal: Zhao Xin counseled the shanyu, saying, “When Han troops cross the desert, men and horses will be exhausted; the Xiongnu can sit and take them prisoner.”
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Xin urged the shanyu: “Once the Han host crosses the desert, men and mounts will be spent—you can bag them at leisure.”

sentences.zh: 乃悉遠北其輜重，皆以精兵待幕北。

translations.literal: He then moved all baggage far to the north and with picked troops awaited them north of the desert.
translations.idiomatic: Accordingly he shifted his baggage train far north and posted elite forces north of the sands to receive them.

sentences.zh: 而適直青軍出塞千餘里，見單于兵陳而待，於是青令武剛車自環為營，而縱五千騎往當匈奴，匈奴亦從萬騎。

translations.literal: It happened that Qing’s army emerged from the passes more than a thousand li and saw the shanyu’s troops drawn up waiting; Qing then ordered war wagons to ring themselves into a camp and loosed five thousand horse to engage the Xiongnu; the Xiongnu likewise sent ten thousand horse.
translations.idiomatic: Qing’s column broke out of the barrier well over a thousand li and found the shanyu’s army formed and waiting. Qing had his armored wagons wheel into a fortified ring, then sent five thousand cavalry to probe the Xiongnu line; the enemy answered with ten thousand riders.

sentences.zh: 會日且入，而大風起，沙礫擊面，兩軍不相見，漢益縱左右翼繞單于。

translations.literal: As the sun was about to set a great wind arose, sand and gravel struck the face, the two armies could not see each other, and Han further loosed left and right wings to circle the shanyu.
translations.idiomatic: Dusk was falling when a gale sprang up, sand blasting their faces until neither side could see. Han then threw both wings wide in an attempt to envelop the shanyu.

sentences.zh: 單于視漢兵多，而士馬尚彊，戰而匈奴不利，薄莫，單于遂乘六臝，壯騎可數百，直冒漢圍西北馳去。

translations.literal: The shanyu saw Han troops were many and men and mounts still strong; fighting, the Xiongnu fared ill; toward dusk the shanyu then mounted six mules, with stalwart horse of several hundred, and went straight through Han’s cordon northwest and fled.
translations.idiomatic: The shanyu saw how numerous the Han were and how fresh their mounts; the Xiongnu line wavered. Near nightfall he sprang onto a team of six mules, surrounded by a few hundred picked horsemen, and bored straight through the Han ring toward the northwest.

sentences.zh: 昏，漢匈奴相紛挐，殺傷大當。

translations.literal: At dusk Han and Xiongnu grappled in confusion; kills and wounds were roughly matched.
translations.idiomatic: In the twilight the two hosts tangled in a confused melee with roughly equal losses.

sentences.zh: 漢軍左校捕虜，言單于未昏而去，漢軍因發輕騎夜追之，青因隨其後。

translations.literal: Han’s left command captured prisoners who said the shanyu had left before dark; Han therefore sent light cavalry in pursuit by night, and Qing followed behind.
translations.idiomatic: Prisoners taken by Han’s left wing reported that the shanyu had quit the field before nightfall; light horse were sent in night pursuit, with Qing trailing the pursuit column.

sentences.zh: 匈奴兵亦散走。

translations.literal: Xiongnu troops also scattered and fled.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu ranks likewise broke and ran.

sentences.zh: 會明，行二百餘里，不得單于，頗捕斬首虜萬餘級，遂至窴顏山趙信城，得匈奴積粟食軍。

translations.literal: By dawn they had gone more than two hundred li, did not catch the shanyu, but took or beheaded over ten thousand grades, then reached Mount Xianyan’s Zhaoxin City, got the Xiongnu’s stored grain, and fed the army.
translations.idiomatic: At daybreak, after more than two hundred li without overtaking the shanyu, they had still chalked up over ten thousand heads. They pushed on to Zhaoxin City below Mount Xianyan, seized the enemy granaries, and fed the troops from them.

sentences.zh: 軍留一日而還，悉燒其城餘粟以歸。

translations.literal: The army halted one day and returned, burning all the city’s surplus grain before withdrawing.
translations.idiomatic: They rested a day, torched the remaining stores in the city, and marched back.

sentences.zh: 青之與單于會也，而前將軍廣、右將軍食其軍別從東道，或失道。

translations.literal: When Qing met the shanyu, van general Guang and right general Shiyi’s army separately followed the eastern road and lost the way.
translations.idiomatic: While Qing engaged the shanyu, van general Li Guang and right general Zhao Shiyi had taken a separate eastern track and gone astray.

sentences.zh: 大將軍引還，過幕南，乃相逢。

translations.literal: The great general led the return, crossed south of the desert, and then they met.
translations.idiomatic: Qing brought his command back across the desert’s southern rim and there linked up with them.

sentences.zh: 青欲使使歸報，令長史簿責廣，廣自殺。

translations.literal: Qing wished to send messengers back to report and ordered the chief clerk to call Guang to account in the registers; Guang killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Qing meant to dispatch a courier to court and told his chief clerk to summon Li Guang for a formal inquiry; Guang took his own life.

sentences.zh: 食其贖為庶人。

translations.literal: Shiyi ransomed his life and became a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Shiyi commuted sentence by ransom and was reduced to a commoner.

sentences.zh: 青軍入塞，凡斬首虜萬九千級。

translations.literal: Qing’s army entered the passes; in all they beheaded or captured 19,000 grades.
translations.idiomatic: When Qing’s army re-entered the barrier, its tally stood at nineteen thousand heads and prisoners.

sentences.zh: 是時匈奴眾失單于十餘日，右谷蠡王自立為單于。

translations.literal: At that time the Xiongnu host had lost the shanyu for more than ten days, and the right Guli king set himself up as shanyu.
translations.idiomatic: For ten days and more the Xiongnu had been without their shanyu; the right Guli king declared himself ruler.

sentences.zh: 單于後得其眾，右王乃去單于之號。

translations.literal: Later the shanyu regathered his host, and the right king then dropped the title of shanyu.
translations.idiomatic: When the true shanyu reassembled his following, the right king quietly abandoned his claim.

sentences.zh: 去病騎兵車重與大將軍軍等，而亡裨將。

translations.literal: Qubing’s cavalry, chariots, and baggage were equal to the great general’s army, but he had no lieutenant general.
translations.idiomatic: Qubing fielded cavalry, wagons, and trains on the same scale as the great general yet commanded no lieutenant generals.

sentences.zh: 悉以李敢等為大校，當裨將，出代、右北平二千餘里，直左方兵，所斬捕功已多於青。

translations.literal: He wholly used Li Gan and others as grand colonels in the place of lieutenant generals, marched out of Dai and Youbeiping more than two thousand li, struck the left-wing host, and the heads he took already exceeded Qing’s.
translations.idiomatic: He relied on Li Gan and others as grand colonels acting as lieutenant generals, drove more than two thousand li from Dai and Youbeiping into the eastern wing, and ran up a butcher’s bill that surpassed Qing’s.

sentences.zh: 既皆還，上曰：「票騎將軍去病率師躬將所獲葷允之士，約輕齎，絕大幕，涉獲單于章渠，以誅北車耆，轉擊左大將雙，獲旗鼓，歷度難侯，濟弓盧，獲屯頭王、韓王等三人，將軍、相國、當戶、都尉八十三人，封狼居胥山，禪於姑衍，登臨翰海，執訊獲醜七萬有四百四十三級，師率減什二，取食於敵，卓行殊遠而糧不絕。

translations.literal: When both had returned, the emperor said, “Swift-cavalry general Qubing led the army, personally commanding warriors of the Xunyu taken captive, traveling light across the great desert, crossed and seized the shanyu’s Zhangqu, executed Beicheqi, turned to strike the left grand general Shuang, captured drums and banners, passed Marquis Dunan, forded Gonglu, captured kings Tuntou and Han among three, eighty-three generals, chancellors, household heads, and commandants, sacrificed on Mount Langjuxu, performed the secondary rite at Guyan, climbed to overlook the Hanhai, took prisoners numbering 70,443 grades, the army lost two tenths of its strength, took food from the enemy, marched extraordinarily far yet never ran short of grain.
translations.idiomatic: When both columns were home, the emperor announced: “The swift-cavalry general led Xunyu tribesmen he had impressed into his ranks, marched light across the desert, crossed the shanyu’s channel warden Zhangqu, slew King Beicheqi, turned on the left grand general Shuang, seized his drums and banners, passed the fief of Marquis Dunan, forded the Gonglu River, captured three kings including Tuntou and Han, together with eighty-three generals, chancellors, household heads, and commandants, sacrificed on Mount Langjuxu, performed the subsidiary rite at Guyan, looked out over the Han sea, took seventy thousand four hundred forty-three living captives, lost only two men in ten, fed his army from the enemy, and pushed farther than any prior column without running out of grain.”

sentences.zh: 以五千八百戶益封票騎將軍。

translations.literal: Increase the swift-cavalry general’s fief by 5,800 households.
translations.idiomatic: Add fifty-eight hundred households to the swift-cavalry general’s fief.

sentences.zh: 右北平太守路博德屬票騎將軍，會興城，不失期，從至檮余山，斬首捕虜二千八百級，封博德為邳離侯。

translations.literal: Prefect of Youbeiping Lu Bode was subordinate to the swift-cavalry general, met at Xingcheng without missing the deadline, followed to Mount Chiyu, beheaded or captured 2,800—enfeoff Bode as marquis of Pilü.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Bode, prefect of Youbeiping, served under the swift-cavalry general, kept his rendezvous at Xingcheng, followed to Mount Chiyu, and accounted for twenty-eight hundred heads—invest Lu Bode as marquis of Pilü.

sentences.zh: 北地都尉衛山從票騎將軍獲王，封山為義陽侯。

translations.literal: Commandant of Beidi Wei Shan followed the swift-cavalry general and captured a king—enfeoff Shan as marquis of Yiyang.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Shan, commandant of Beidi, rode with the swift-cavalry general and seized a king—invest him as marquis of Yiyang.

sentences.zh: 故歸義侯因淳王復陸支、樓剸王伊即靬皆從票騎將軍有功，封復陸支為杜侯，伊即靬為眾利侯。

translations.literal: Former marquis of submission King Fuzhizhi of the Yichun and King Yijian of the Loujian both followed the swift-cavalry general with merit—enfeoff Fuzhizhi as marquis of Du and Yijian as marquis of Zhongli.
translations.idiomatic: The former submission marquises—Fuzhizhi of the Yichun and Yijian of the Loujian—both distinguished themselves under the swift-cavalry general; Fuzhizhi became marquis of Du, Yijian marquis of Zhongli.

sentences.zh: 從票侯破奴、昌武侯安稽從票騎有功，益封各三百戶。

translations.literal: Marquis attendant on the ticket Ponu and Marquis of Changwu Anji followed the swift-cavalry with merit—increase each’s fief by 300 households.
translations.idiomatic: Ponu the marquis attendant on the ticket and Anji the Marquis of Changwu both earned merit with the swift-cavalry general; each received three hundred added households.

sentences.zh: 漁陽太守解、校尉敢皆獲鼓旗，賜爵關內侯，解食邑三百戶，敢二百戶。

translations.literal: Prefect of Yuyang Xie and Colonel Gan both captured drums and banners and were granted rank as marquis within the passes; Xie received three hundred households of revenue, Gan two hundred.
translations.idiomatic: The Yuyang prefect surnamed Xie and Colonel Li Gan each took enemy drums and standards and were made marquises within the passes—three hundred households for Xie, two hundred for Gan.

sentences.zh: 校尉自為爵左庶長。」

translations.literal: Colonel Ziwei was granted rank as left chief of the people.”
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Ziwei was raised to left chief of the people.”

sentences.zh: 軍吏卒為官，賞賜甚多。

translations.literal: Officers and soldiers became officials; rewards and gifts were very many.
translations.idiomatic: Officers and men were promoted and heaped with rewards.

sentences.zh: 而青不得益封，吏卒無封者。

translations.literal: But Qing did not receive an increased fief, and no officers or soldiers under him were enfeoffed.
translations.idiomatic: Qing himself received no addition to his fief, and none of his subordinates were given new marquisates.

sentences.zh: 唯西河太守常惠、雲中太守遂成受賞，遂成秩諸侯相，賜食邑二百戶，黃金百斤，惠爵關內侯。

translations.literal: Only West River prefect Chang Hui and Yunzhong prefect Suicheng received rewards; Suicheng’s salary was raised to that of a feudal chancellor, he was granted two hundred households of revenue and a hundred pounds of gold, and Hui was given rank as marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: Only Chang Hui of West River and Suicheng of Yunzhong were singled out—Suicheng’s stipend was lifted to a feudal chancellor’s level, with two hundred households and a hundred pounds of gold, while Hui received the rank of marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 兩軍之出塞，塞閱官及私馬凡十四萬匹，而後入塞者不滿三萬匹。

translations.literal: Of the two armies’ crossing of the barrier, government and private horses inspected at the passes totaled 140,000, but those that re-entered the passes afterward were fewer than 30,000.
translations.idiomatic: The two hosts had filed out with a hundred forty thousand government and private mounts past the barrier muster; fewer than thirty thousand animals came back in.

sentences.zh: 乃置大司馬位，大將軍、票騎將軍皆為大司馬。

translations.literal: He then established the office of grand marshal; the great general and swift-cavalry general both became grand marshals.
translations.idiomatic: The court then created the post of grand marshal, and both the great general and the swift-cavalry general were named to it.

sentences.zh: 定令，令票騎將軍秩祿與大將軍等。

translations.literal: He fixed the statutes so that the swift-cavalry general’s rank and salary equaled the great general’s.
translations.idiomatic: Regulations were revised so that the swift-cavalry general’s grade and pay matched the great general’s.

sentences.zh: 自是後，青日衰而去病日益貴。

translations.literal: From this time Qing daily declined while Qubing daily grew more honored.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward Qing’s star waned as Qubing’s rose.

sentences.zh: 青故人門下多去事去病，輒得官爵，唯獨任安不肯去。

translations.literal: Many of Qing’s old friends and retainers went to serve Qubing and promptly received office and rank; only Ren An refused to go.
translations.idiomatic: Former clients drifted from Qing’s gate to Qubing’s and walked away with titles; only Ren An stayed loyal.

sentences.zh: 去病為人少言不泄，有氣敢往。

translations.literal: Qubing as a man spoke little and did not leak secrets; he had spirit and dared to go forward.
translations.idiomatic: Qubing was reticent and close-mouthed, but bold and decisive once in the field.

sentences.zh: 上嘗欲教之吳孫兵法，對曰：「顧方略何如耳，不至學古兵法。」

translations.literal: The emperor once wished to teach him the Wu and Sunzi arts of war; he replied, “It depends on strategy—there is no need to study ancient military texts.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor once offered to tutor him in the Wu and Sunzi classics; he answered, “Victory turns on planning, not on poring over old manuals.”

sentences.zh: 上為治第，令視之，對曰：「匈奴不滅，無以家為也。」

translations.literal: The emperor built him a mansion and bade him inspect it; he replied, “The Xiongnu are not yet destroyed—there is no time to think of a household.”
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor had a fine house built for him and asked his opinion, he said, “Until the Xiongnu are gone, I have no business tending a private home.”

sentences.zh: 由此上益重愛之。

translations.literal: From this the emperor increasingly esteemed and loved him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor only prized him the more for it.

sentences.zh: 然少而侍中，貴不省士。

translations.literal: Yet from youth he was a palace attendant, and in his eminence he did not pity the common soldier.
translations.idiomatic: Raised young inside the palace, he never learned sympathy for the rank and file.

sentences.zh: 其從軍，上為遣太官齎數十乘，既還，重車餘棄粱肉，而士有飢者。

translations.literal: When he campaigned the emperor sent the grand provisioner with several tens of wagons; when he returned, surplus grain carts threw away good grain and meat while soldiers went hungry.
translations.idiomatic: On campaign the throne sent the imperial kitchen after him with dozens of supply carts; on his return he left wagons full of spoiled grain and meat while his men starved.

sentences.zh: 其在塞外，卒乏糧，或不能自振，而去病尚穿域鸲鞠也。

translations.literal: When he was beyond the barrier, the troops lacked grain and some could not pull themselves up, yet Qubing still marked out a pitch and played cuju.
translations.idiomatic: Beyond the wall, with men too weak from hunger to stand, he would still stake out a field and kick a ball.

sentences.zh: 事多此類。

translations.literal: Matters were mostly of this sort.
translations.idiomatic: His conduct was often of that kind.

sentences.zh: 青仁，喜士退讓，以和柔自媚於上，然於天下未有稱也。

translations.literal: Qing was humane, delighted in gentlemen and yielding, and by mildness flattered the sovereign, yet in the realm there was none who praised him.
translations.idiomatic: Qing was kindly, cultivated scholars, and deferred to others, winning favor through gentleness—yet the world offered him little praise.

sentences.zh: 自去病死後，青長子宜春侯伉坐法失侯。

translations.literal: After Qubing died, Qing’s eldest son Kang the marquis of Yichun lost his marquisate for violating the law.
translations.idiomatic: After Qubing’s death, Qing’s eldest son Kang, marquis of Yichun, forfeited his title for a legal offense.

sentences.zh: 後五歲，伉弟二人，陰安侯不疑、發干侯登，皆坐酎伉失侯。

translations.literal: Five years later Kang’s two younger brothers—Buyi marquis of Yin’an and Deng marquis of Fagan—both lost their marquisates sitting for the libation gold offense implicating Kang.
translations.idiomatic: Five years later Kang’s younger brothers—Buyi, marquis of Yin’an, and Deng, marquis of Fagan—also forfeited their titles when the communal-wine-gold inquest implicated Kang’s line.

sentences.zh: 後二歲，冠軍侯國絕。

translations.literal: Two years later the Champion marquisate line was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Two years after that the Champion marquisate died out for lack of an heir.

sentences.zh: 後四年，元封五年，青薨，諡曰烈侯。

translations.literal: Four years later, in the fifth year of the Yuanfeng era, Qing died and was posthumously titled marquis Lie.
translations.idiomatic: Four years later, in the fifth year of the Yuanfeng era (106 BCE), Qing died and received the posthumous title Marquis Lie.

sentences.zh: 子伉嗣，六年坐法免。

translations.literal: His son Kang succeeded; in the sixth year he lost office for violating the law.
translations.idiomatic: His son Kang inherited the title but was stripped in the sixth year for a legal infraction.

sentences.zh: 自青圍單于後十四歲而卒，竟不復擊匈奴者，以漢馬少，又方南誅兩越，東伐朝鮮，擊羌、西南夷，以故久不伐胡。

translations.literal: From Qing’s encirclement of the shanyu to his death was fourteen years; in the end Han did not again strike the Xiongnu because Han horses were few, and moreover it was then executing the two Yue in the south, attacking Joseon in the east, and striking the Qiang and southwestern Yi—therefore for long it did not attack the Hu.
translations.idiomatic: Fourteen years passed between Qing’s envelopment of the shanyu and his death; Han launched no further major strikes because horse stocks had dwindled, while campaigns consumed the south against both Yue, the east against Joseon, and the interior against the Qiang and southwestern tribes—so the northern frontier stayed quiet for years.

sentences.zh: 初，青既尊貴，而平陽侯曹壽有惡疾就國，長公主問：「列侯誰賢者？」

translations.literal: At first, when Qing had already become honored, Marquis of Pingyang Cao Shou had a vile illness and went to his fief; the Grand Princess asked, “Among the marquises who is worthy?”
translations.idiomatic: Early in Qing’s eminence the Marquis of Pingyang, Cao Shou, fell gravely ill and retired to his estate. The Grand Princess asked her attendants, “Which of the marquises is worth taking?”

sentences.zh: 左右皆言大將軍。

translations.literal: Those at her side all said the great general.
translations.idiomatic: Every voice named the great general.

sentences.zh: 主笑曰：「此出吾家，常騎從我，柰何？」

translations.literal: The princess laughed and said, “This one came out of my household and used to ride in attendance on me—how can that be?”
translations.idiomatic: She laughed and said, “He was raised in my own house and used to ride at my stirrup—how could I marry him?”

sentences.zh: 左右曰：「於今尊貴無比。」

translations.literal: Those at her side said, “Today none is more honored.”
translations.idiomatic: They answered, “No man in the realm now ranks higher.”

sentences.zh: 於是長公主風白皇后，皇后言之，上乃詔青尚平陽主，與主合葬，起冢象廬山云。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Grand Princess hinted to the empress, the empress spoke to the emperor, and the emperor then ordered Qing to marry the Princess of Pingyang; they were buried together, and a tumulus was raised modeled on Mount Lu.
translations.idiomatic: She dropped a hint to the empress, who spoke to the throne; an edict then joined Qing in marriage to the Princess of Pingyang. They were laid in a shared tomb mound shaped to echo Mount Lu.

sentences.zh: 最大將軍青凡七出擊匈奴，斬捕首虜五萬餘級。

translations.literal: In sum Great General Qing seven times went out to strike the Xiongnu and beheaded or captured more than fifty thousand grades.
translations.idiomatic: Great General Qing campaigned against the Xiongnu seven times and accounted for more than fifty thousand heads and prisoners.

sentences.zh: 一與單于戰，收河南地，置朔方郡。

translations.literal: Once he fought the shanyu, recovered the land south of the river, and established Shuofang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He met the shanyu in battle once, reclaimed the Ordos south of the Yellow River, and founded Shuofang commandery.

sentences.zh: 再益封，凡萬六千三百戶；

translations.literal: Twice he received additions to his fief, in all 16,300 households;
translations.idiomatic: His fief was enlarged twice, for a total of sixteen thousand three hundred households;

sentences.zh: 封三子為侯，侯千三百戶，并之二萬二百戶。

translations.literal: he enfeoffed three sons as marquises, each marquisate 1,300 households, together with the previous making 20,200 households.
translations.idiomatic: three sons were enfeoffed at thirteen hundred households each, bringing the combined household count to twenty thousand two hundred.

sentences.zh: 其裨將及校尉侯者九人，為特將者十五人，李廣、張騫、公孫賀、李蔡、曹襄、韓說、蘇建皆自有傳。

translations.literal: His lieutenant generals and colonels who were enfeoffed were nine; those who served as independent generals were fifteen; Li Guang, Zhang Qian, Gongsun He, Li Cai, Cao Xiang, Han Shuo, and Su Jian each have their own biography.
translations.idiomatic: Nine lieutenant generals and colonels under him won marquisates; fifteen rose to independent command. Li Guang, Zhang Qian, Gongsun He, Li Cai, Cao Xiang, Han Shuo, and Su Jian are treated in separate chapters.

sentences.zh: 李息，郁郅人也，事景帝。

translations.literal: Li Xi was a man of Yuzhi and served Emperor Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xi came from Yuzhi and had served Emperor Jing.

sentences.zh: 至武帝立八歲，為材官將軍，軍馬邑；

translations.literal: When eight years had passed since Emperor Wu’s accession, he became timber-and-barracks general and encamped at Mayi;
translations.idiomatic: Eight years into Emperor Wu’s reign he was made timber-and-barracks general and stationed at Mayi;

sentences.zh: 後六歲，為將軍，出代；

translations.literal: six years later he was general and marched out of Dai;
translations.idiomatic: six years after that he held a general’s commission and advanced from Dai;

sentences.zh: 後三歲，為將軍，從大將軍出朔方：皆無功。

translations.literal: three years later he was general and followed the great general out of Shuofang: all without merit.
translations.idiomatic: three years later he was again a general and followed the great general from Shuofang—each time without distinction.

sentences.zh: 凡三為將軍，其後常為大行。

translations.literal: In all he thrice was general; afterward he constantly was grand messenger.
translations.idiomatic: He served three tours as a general, then spent the remainder of his career as grand messenger.

sentences.zh: 公孫敖

translations.literal: Gongsun Ao (subsection heading).
translations.idiomatic: The following subsection treats Gongsun Ao.

sentences.zh: 公孫敖，義渠人，以郎事景帝。

translations.literal: Gongsun Ao was a man of Yiqu and as a gentleman-attendant served Emperor Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Ao came from Yiqu and had served Emperor Jing as a gentleman-attendant.

sentences.zh: 至武帝立十二歲，為騎將軍，出代，亡卒七千人，當斬，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: When twelve years had passed since Emperor Wu’s accession, he was cavalry general, marched out of Dai, lost seven thousand soldiers, was liable for execution, and ransomed his life to become a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: Twelve years into Emperor Wu’s reign he led a cavalry column out of Dai, lost seven thousand men, faced capital sentence, and bought his way down to commoner status.

sentences.zh: 後五歲，以校尉從大將軍，封合騎侯。

translations.literal: Five years later, as colonel he followed the great general and was enfeoffed as marquis of Heqi.
translations.idiomatic: Five years later he rode with the great general as a colonel and was invested as marquis of Heqi.

sentences.zh: 後一歲，以中將軍從大將軍再出定襄，無功。

translations.literal: One year later, as central general he followed the great general in again marching out of Dingxiang—without merit.
translations.idiomatic: The next year he served as central general on a second Dingxiang expedition under the great general and won no distinction.

sentences.zh: 後二歲，以將軍出北地，後票騎，失期當斬，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: Two years later, as general he marched out of Beidi, lagged behind the swift-cavalry, missed the deadline, was liable for execution, and ransomed his life to become a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: Two years after that he held an independent command from Beidi, fell behind the swift-cavalry column, missed his rendezvous, faced death, and ransomed out to a commoner.

sentences.zh: 後二歲，以校尉從大將軍，無功。

translations.literal: Two years later, as colonel he followed the great general—without merit.
translations.idiomatic: Two years later he again followed the great general as a colonel and achieved nothing notable.

sentences.zh: 後十四歲，以因杅將軍築受降城。

translations.literal: Fourteen years later, as Yangan general he built the city for receiving surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Fourteen years on, as Yangan general he constructed the fortress for receiving capitulations.

sentences.zh: 七歲，復以因杅將軍再出擊匈奴，至余吾，亡士多，下吏，當斬，詐死，亡居民間五六歲。

translations.literal: Seven years later, again as Yangan general he twice went out to strike the Xiongnu, reached Yuwu, lost very many soldiers, was handed to the judicial officials, was liable for execution, feigned death, and fled to hide among the people five or six years.
translations.idiomatic: Seven years later he once more held the Yangan commission, campaigned twice against the Xiongnu as far as Yuwu, shed troops on a ruinous scale, was remanded for trial, faced execution, faked his death, and lived as a fugitive among commoners for five or six years.

sentences.zh: 後覺，復繫。

translations.literal: Later it was discovered and he was again bound.
translations.idiomatic: When the deception was uncovered he was seized again.

sentences.zh: 坐妻為巫蠱，族。

translations.literal: He sat because his wife practiced witchcraft and sorcery—his whole clan was exterminated.
translations.idiomatic: He was destroyed in the witchcraft purge: his wife had been implicated, and the entire lineage was executed.

sentences.zh: 凡四為將軍。

translations.literal: In all he four times was general.
translations.idiomatic: He held a general’s commission on four occasions.

sentences.zh: 李沮，雲中人，事景帝。

translations.literal: Li Ju was a man of Yunzhong and served Emperor Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ju came from Yunzhong and had served Emperor Jing.

sentences.zh: 武帝立十七歲，以左內史為彊弩將軍。

translations.literal: When seventeen years had passed since Emperor Wu’s accession, as director of the left interior he was strong crossbow general.
translations.idiomatic: Seventeen years into Emperor Wu’s reign he left the left interior to become strong-crossbow general.

sentences.zh: 後一歲，復為彊弩將軍。

translations.literal: One year later, again he was strong crossbow general.
translations.idiomatic: The following year he served a second tour as strong-crossbow general.

sentences.zh: 張次公

translations.literal: Zhang Cigong (subsection heading).
translations.idiomatic: The following subsection treats Zhang Cigong.

sentences.zh: 張次公，河東人，以校尉從大將軍，封岸頭侯。

translations.literal: Zhang Cigong was a man of Hedong; as colonel he followed the great general and was enfeoffed as marquis of Antou.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cigong of Hedong followed the great general as a colonel and was invested as marquis of Antou.

sentences.zh: 其後太后崩，為將軍，軍北軍。

translations.literal: Afterward when the empress dowager died, he became general and encamped the northern army.
translations.idiomatic: After the empress dowager’s death he took command as general of the Northern Army garrison.

sentences.zh: 後一歲，復從大將軍。

translations.literal: One year later, again he followed the great general.
translations.idiomatic: A year later he campaigned again under the great general.

sentences.zh: 凡再為將軍，後坐法失侯。

translations.literal: In all he twice was general; afterward he lost his marquisate sitting for violating the law.
translations.idiomatic: He twice held general’s rank, then forfeited his marquisate for a legal offense.

sentences.zh: 趙信，以匈奴相國降，為侯。

translations.literal: Zhao Xin, because he was Xiongnu chancellor and surrendered, became a marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Xin had been a Xiongnu chancellor who defected and received a marquisate.
